text.title: Stakeholder Register Data Schema and Agent Gotchas
text.summary: YAML data schema for the stakeholder register, privacy handling rules, and agent-specific pitfalls in stakeholder identification and attitude classification.

example.code:
- id: S-01 name: Olena Kravchenko role: VP Engineering dept: Engineering interests: - delivery cadence - tech-debt reduction influence: H # H | M | L impact: H attitude: supportive # supportive | neutral | resistant | unknown quadrant: manage-closely # manage-closely | keep-satisfied | keep-informed | monitor comms: cadence: weekly channel: 1:1 + Slack DM notes: Sponsor for Q2 platform initiative. last_touch: 2026-04-22
- id: S-02 name: Security Council role: Group dept: Security interests: [compliance, audit-trail] influence: H impact: M attitude: resistant quadrant: keep-satisfied comms: cadence: bi-weekly channel: review meeting last_touch: 2026-04-15
example.note: Quadrant is auto-computed from influence × interest but stored explicitly so changes are visible in diffs.

rule.statement: Store attitude as "unknown" when no evidence exists in inputs — never invent an attitude.
rule.rationale: An agent that infers "supportive" from polite email tone produces a false register. Unknown flags the PM to gather direct evidence before trusting the classification.

rule.statement: Keep PII (email, phone, salary) in a separate access-controlled file; the in-repo register holds only ID and role.
rule.rationale: A stakeholder register committed to a shared repo exposes sensitive contact and compensation data. Separation allows broad repo access while restricting PII to those who need it.

list.item: Do not publish the full register to stakeholders themselves — attitude fields are internal; redact before sharing
Apply the same access control tier as the project charter (executive-only for High-Power stakeholders)

rule.statement: Require attitude evidence as a quoted string from source material; reject any attitude assignment without a quote.
rule.rationale: Agents systematically over-classify as "Supportive" because meeting transcripts and emails are written in professional, polite language. Requiring a quoted source prevents pattern-matching on tone.

list.item: Agents extract names from email signatures and infer seniority from job title — many blockers are mid-level gatekeepers (procurement, security review)
Group stakeholders get split into individuals if you ask the LLM to "list people" — preserve the group when authority is collective
Stale registers go undetected: build a last_touch field and a weekly cron diff vs comms logs
Mid-level "influencer" stakeholders are systematically under-rated; explicitly prompt for "who has a veto even if not in the org chart?"
